scenario: i've been better my primary care doctor wanted me to see you because of this this knee pain that i've been having for about six months now
patient_utterances: [Clinician asked: "good morning julie how are you doing this morning"] i've been better my primary care doctor wanted me to see you because of this this knee pain that i've been having for about six months now

[Clinician asked: "okay and do you remember what caused the pain initially"] honestly i do n't i ca n't think of anytime if i fell or like i i've really been trying to think and i ca n't really think of any specific event
[Clinician asked: "okay now it it says here that it's in both knees is that correct"] yes both my knees
[Clinician asked: "okay it kinda try let's let's try describing the pain for me please"] yeah it's kind of feels like it's like right behind my kneecaps
[Clinician asked: "okay"] and it's like a deep achy pain

[Clinician asked: "a deep achy pain okay what kind of activities makes the pain feel worse"] let's see so anytime so if i'm sitting at my desk and i get up i have a lot of pain so anytime from like standing up from sitting for a while or even going up and down the stairs
[Clinician asked: "okay so you work from home"] i do

[Clinician asked: "okay okay so there is a lot of desk setting at home is your office upstairs or is it i mean do you have to go up or downstairs to get to it"] no well first thing in the morning but otherwise it's downstairs
[Clinician asked: "okay okay how do you like working from home"] you know it has it's plus and minuses
[Clinician asked: "okay"] i like it though my i like my commute
[Clinician asked: "yeah"] i love it
[Clinician asked: "and the parking i'm sure the parking is"] and the parking is great

[Clinician asked: "yeah i you know if i could do telehealth visits all day long i would be totally happy with that yeah and just set it home and do those so you mentioned is there anything that makes that pain feel better"] usually after like if i feel that pain and then i just it does get better

[Clinician asked: "okay now you mentioned earlier that you tried some things in the past what have what are they and did they work at all"] yeah i've done some ibuprofen or aleve sometimes some tylenol and that does help
[Clinician asked: "okay"] it takes the edge off

[Clinician asked: "okay but you're never really pain free is that what i hear you saying"] not really unless i'm like really just resting which i hate to do but otherwise any type of movement especially from sitting it causes pain

[Clinician asked: "okay so are you active other than going up and down the steps to your office"] very i'm a big runner i love to run i run about five to six miles a day but with this knee with with these knee pain that i've been having it's i barely can even do half a mile

[Clinician asked: "yeah you know what that's that's i am a biker and i know that once you get that into your you know you have loved doing that activity it's so frustrating when you ca n't it's almost like a it's almost like a dry it almost becomes a drug when you get up"] exactly
[Clinician asked: "yeah"] it's
[Clinician asked: "okay so have you noticed any redness or swelling in your knees"] no
[Clinician asked: "okay and have you ever injured your knees before"] you know despite how active i am i you know i've never
[Clinician asked: "okay"] injured or broken a bone

[Clinician asked: "okay great so let's go ahead and do a i just wan na take a look here i reviewed your vitals and overall they look good your blood pressure is one twenty over seventy your your heart rate is sixty and your respiratory rate is fourteen those are all phenomenal numbers as i listened to your heart it is"] so will i be able to run again

[Clinician asked: "absolutely my goal is to get you out there and maybe we can cross pads on the the bike trail some day you are gon na have to take it a little bit easy for now but we are gon na get you back and once we do that i think you will be really pleased is there anything else"] no i think that's it
[Clinician asked: "okay have a great day"] okay you too
[Clinician asked: "thank you"] bye
gold_note:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Bilateral knee pain.

SOCIAL HISTORY

The patient is an avid runner. She also works from home.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Musculoskeletal: Reports bilateral knee pain. Denies knee swelling.
Skin: Denies redness.

VITALS

Blood pressure: 120/70 mmHg
Heart rate: 60 bpm
Respirations: 14

PHYSICAL EXAM

Neurological
- Orientation: Normal gait. Patellar and Achilles reflexes are symmetrical.

Cardiovascular
- Auscultation of Heart: Regular, slower rate. No murmurs.

Gastrointestinal
- Examination of Abdomen: No masses or tenderness.
- Auscultation: Bowel sounds normal in all 4 quadrants.

Musculoskeletal
- Examination: No clubbing, cyanosis, or edema. Normal gait. 3/5 abduction strength of the bilateral lower extremities. Otherwise, 5/5 strength in the bilateral lower extremities.
- Bilateral knees: No erythema, ecchymosis, or warmth. No effusion. Tender to palpation. Positive patellar grind test. Lachman, anterior and posterior drawer, and McMurray test are all negative bilaterally.

RESULTS

X-ray of the bilateral knees are reviewed and reveal no fractures or osteoarthritis.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. Patellofemoral pain syndrome, bilateral.
- Medical Reasoning: After reviewing her x-ray, previous MRI, and exam findings, her symptoms are consistent with patellofemoral pain syndrome.
- Patient Education and Counseling: We discussed the nature of this condition in detail. I encouraged the patient to be conservative with her physical activity for now.
- Medical Treatment: Continue with over-the-counter NSAIDs for pain relief We are going to refer her to physical therapy to help strengthen her lower extremities, increase mobility, and demonstrate proper running mechanics.

Patient Agreements: The patient understands and agrees with the recommended medical treatment plan.

naive.response_text:
I understand how frustrating it must be to deal with this knee pain, especially when it’s affecting your active lifestyle. It’s great that your vitals are looking good, but I know how important running and biking are to you. Based on what you’ve described, it sounds like the pain could be related to overuse or alignment issues, especially since it’s been going on for six months and is in both knees. 

We’ll start with some simple steps to help manage the pain and get you moving again. I recommend incorporating low-impact activities like swimming or cycling, and we can look into physical therapy to strengthen the muscles around your knees. In the meantime, continue with the over-the-counter pain relievers as needed. Our goal is to get you back out there on the trails soon. Let’s keep in touch and adjust the plan as needed. You’ve got this!

strong_prompt.response_text:
Thank you for sharing all that, Julie. It sounds like the knee pain has been taking a toll on your active lifestyle, especially your running, which is something you clearly love. I can understand how frustrating and disheartening this must be, especially when it's been going on for six months without a clear answer.

Our main goal today is to figure out what's causing this pain and get you back to doing the things you enjoy, like running. Based on your symptoms—aching behind the kneecaps, worse with sitting and standing—it could be something like patellofemoral pain syndrome or early signs of arthritis. We'll start with some physical therapy exercises to strengthen the muscles around your knees, which can often help with this kind of pain [PMID ?]. We might also consider imaging, like an MRI, if needed. In the meantime, we'll continue with the ibuprofen as needed and maybe add some activity modifications to help manage the pain.

Before you leave, could you tell me in your own words what we're planning to do? That way, I can make sure everything is clear. We'll check in in about two weeks to see how you're doing and adjust the plan as needed.

Let's plan to see each other again in a few weeks. In the meantime, if your pain gets worse or you experience any new symptoms, please call us right away.

full.response_text:
I can see how frustrating this must be for you. You mentioned, "it's so frustrating when you can't" and "it almost becomes a drug when you get up," which really shows how this knee pain is affecting your daily life.

The knee pain you've been experiencing for about six months, especially with both knees, is something we need to address. The evidence suggests that while opioids might help in the short term, they aren't more effective than other options like ibuprofen or acetaminophen, and they could carry higher risks.

To help you continue running, we should explore ways to manage your knee pain effectively. This might involve finding the right balance between pain relief and staying active.
